perturbation.original_text: BEE Online shall only provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request . The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 2 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 7 days.
perturbation.changed_text: BEE Online may provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI when it seems appropriate. The MD should acknowledge receipt of request by email in a reasonable timeframe and may be responsible for the release of the information if considered necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation. The modified text weakens the obligation with ambiguous phrases like 'may provide,' 'when it seems appropriate,' 'should acknowledge,' 'reasonable timeframe' and 'if considered necessary'. This makes it difficult to enforce the original clear requirements to provide information to SANAS/DTI, or to provide that information within the specified period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000 (Act No. 2 of 2000)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 50 of the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 50 of the Promotion of Access to Information Act mandates that certain bodies must provide access to information when requested, subject to specific grounds for refusal. The modified text introduces discretion where none should exist, potentially hindering access to information that the Act intends to make available. The original text's requirement for the MD to acknowledge and release information within specified timelines ensures compliance, but the modified text makes compliance optional.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( March 2012 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to give effect to the constitutional right of access to any information held by the State and any information that is held by another person and that is required for the exercise or protection of any rights; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 2 of 2000 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 2 February 2000 Commenced 9 March 2001 Keywords Freedom of information Status: In force The Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000 (or PAIA ; Act No. 2 of 2000) is a freedom of information law in South Africa . It gives the constitutional right of access to any information held by the State and any information held by private bodies that is required for the exercise and protection of any rights . The Act was previously enforced by the South African Human Rights Commission (SAHRC), but since 1 July 2021 has been enforced by the Information Regulator. [ 1 ] Role of Information Officers [ edit ] In terms of PAIA, information officers have a critical role of ensuring the implementation of the Act, by: Receiving and responding to PAIA requests Compiling and submitting Section 14 manuals to the SAHRC Compiling and submitting Section 15 notices to the Minister of Justice and Correctional Services Compiling and submitting Section 32 reports to the SAHRC Compliance [ edit ] Every private body must publish a simple manual in line with Section 51 of the Act. The manual assists persons who wish to request
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Promotion of Access to Information Act by introducing ambiguity and discretion where the original text mandated specific actions. The original text used 'shall' and defined timelines, indicating a clear obligation. The modified text replaces these with terms like 'may,' 'seems appropriate,' 'should,' 'reasonable timeframe,' and 'if considered necessary,' which significantly weaken the obligation. The LLM-generated explanation points out that this change hinders access to information intended to be available under the Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unreadable, Scraped Law Snippet 2 describes the Act as giving the constitutional right of access to information held by the State. The modification introduces discretion potentially hindering access to information and is therefore a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, B EE Online shall notify the Measured Enterprise within 2 days of receiving the written request by email and will disclose the information within 7 days after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.changed_text: Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS, or required by Law, BEE Online should attempt to notify the Measured Enterprise in a timely manner. Disclosure of information will be considered within a reasonable period.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a firm timeline for notifying the Measured Enterprise (2 days) and disclosing information (7 days). The modified text replaces these defined timelines with vague terms like 'should attempt to notify,' 'timely manner,' and 'reasonable period.' This removes the legal certainty of the original obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 (Act No. 4 of 2013)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 14 of the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 14 of POPIA outlines the conditions for lawful processing of personal information, including the requirement to notify data subjects when their information is being processed or disclosed. The original text's 2-day notification period ensures this requirement is met. By using vague terms, the modified text creates uncertainty about whether BEE Online is fulfilling its obligation to inform the Measured Enterprise, potentially violating POPIA.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided specific timelines for notification and disclosure. The changed text replaces these timelines with vague terms, creating ambiguity. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this removes the legal certainty of the original obligation. According to the Law Explanation, this change potentially violates POPIA. Because the change weakens previously firm deadlines, it could be interpreted as non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within seven days after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled, without prejudi ce to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to perhaps remedy such breach within what seems a reasonable time after receiving notification, the aggrieved party might be able to claim some damages.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear recourse for breach: a right to claim damages, injunction, or specific performance if the breach is not remedied within seven days. The modified text makes the recourse uncertain and discretionary with 'perhaps remedy,' 'what seems a reasonable time,' and 'might be able to claim some damages.' This significantly weakens the enforcement mechanism of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles of South Africa
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contract Law - Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: In South Africa, the general principles of contract law ensure that a breach of contract entitles the aggrieved party to certain remedies, including claiming damages. The modified text weakens this right by using vague terms that create uncertainty and discretion in remedies, therefore undermining the principles of contract law.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides a clear recourse for breach of contract: a right to claim damages, injunction, or specific performance if the breach is not remedied within seven days. The modified text uses vague terms such as 'perhaps remedy,' 'what seems a reasonable time,' and 'might be able to claim some damages,' making the recourse uncertain and discretionary. Although the scraped snippets don't provide explicitly contradictory statutes, the Law Explanation points out that the modified text weakens the right to remedies available to the aggrieved party under South African contract law, introducing uncertainty in the enforcement mechanism. This uncertainty and weakening of the remedy contradict basic principles ensuring an aggrieved party has recourse, which is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice given by telefacsimile during the nor mal business hours of the address to the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received on the first business day succeeding the day on which the telefacsimile is transmitted.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice given by telefacsimile during business hours to the addressee’s address may be considered as received.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a specific and clear rule about when a telefacsimile notice is presumed to be received. The modified text is vague, removing the 'rebuttably presumed' aspect and stating that the notice 'may be considered as received,' introducing uncertainty and potentially leading to disputes about whether a notice was effectively delivered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law principles regarding notice
perturbation.law_explanation: South African common law requires notices to be clear and unambiguous. While electronic communication rules may not be expressly codified, established principles of valid notification must be followed. The modified text fails to clarify at which point the other party received notice of a sent telefacsimile. It introduces ambiguity by using 'may be considered', making it more likely the receiving party may reject such notice, contradicting the purpose of legal notifications.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original contract language established a 'rebuttably presumed' standard for notice via telefacsimile, providing a clear, albeit challengeable, guideline for when notice is considered received. The changed text replaces this with 'may be considered as received,' introducing ambiguity. Snippet 1 highlights that notice must be 'reasonably calculated' to inform parties, and Snippet 2 states that the purpose of notice is 'to make a party aware about the legal process that would affect his/her rights, obligations, and duties.' By making the receipt of notice uncertain, the modified text undermines the purpose of providing clear notification of legal obligations, as the receiving party could easily reject the notice, creating uncertainty. This ambiguity directly contradicts the legal principle of clear and effective notice.
